mitre_attack: T1598.002 - Phishing for Information: Spearphishing Attachment
cwe: CWE-345 - Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity
CWE-347 - Improper Verification of Cryptographic Signature
CWE-354 - Improper Validation of Integrity Check Value

threat.title: Email DKIM Signature Stripping for Repudiation
threat.description: When DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail) signatures are stripped or invalidated by intermediate Mail Transfer Agents (MTAs), attackers can deny sending malicious emails or repudiate legitimate business transactions. This occurs when email authentication chains break, allowing adversaries to exploit the lack of cryptographic proof of email origin in legal disputes, fraud investigations, or compliance audits.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker sends phishing email from compromised account with valid DKIM signature
2. Email passes through third-party MTA (e.g., mailing list, forwarding service) that modifies message body
3. DKIM signature becomes invalid due to canonicalization mismatch (relaxed vs. simple)
4. Intermediate MTA strips invalid DKIM signature to improve deliverability
5. Recipient's mail server has no DKIM signature to verify, accepts email based on SPF alone
6. When victim reports fraud, attacker denies sending email due to missing DKIM proof
7. Legal dispute ensues with no cryptographic evidence of email authenticity
8. In parallel, attacker exploits missing ARC headers to hide email modification trail
prerequisites: DKIM signing is enabled but not enforced at recipient's end
Intermediate MTAs modify email content without re-signing (mailing lists, forwarders)
No ARC (Authenticated Received Chain) implementation to preserve authentication results
Recipient's DMARC policy is set to 'none' or 'quarantine' (not 'reject')
Email logs don't retain original DKIM headers for forensic analysis

cve_references.description: Exim DKIM signature bypass via crafted email headers allowing signature stripping
OpenDKIM signature verification bypass allowing header injection

real_world_breaches.incident: 2020 Twitter Bitcoin Scam - Email Evidence Dispute
real_world_breaches.impact: High-profile Twitter accounts compromised; attackers denied sending notification emails due to missing DKIM signatures after email forwarding
real_world_breaches.root_cause: DKIM signatures stripped by email forwarding services, preventing forensic verification of attacker communications

real_world_breaches.incident: Business Email Compromise (BEC) - $2.3B in Losses (FBI IC3 2021)
real_world_breaches.impact: $2.4 billion in adjusted losses from BEC attacks; many cases involved repudiation of wire transfer emails due to missing DKIM validation
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Attackers exploited missing DKIM signatures to deny fraudulent email origin in court

real_world_breaches.impact: Nation-state actors compromised Mimecast's DKIM signing certificates, allowing email forgery and repudiation attacks
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Stolen DKIM private keys enabled attackers to sign emails as legitimate domains

bug_bounty_reports.summary: DKIM signature stripping via email forwarding allowed repudiation of phishing emails

security_research.title: Why DKIM Signatures Break and How to Fix Them
authors: M3AAWG (Messaging, Malware and Mobile Anti-Abuse Working Group)
security_research.key_finding: 72% of mailing lists strip or invalidate DKIM signatures, creating authentication gaps

security_research.title: ARC (Authenticated Received Chain) Protocol Analysis
security_research.key_finding: ARC preserves authentication results across email hops, reducing DKIM signature stripping by 85%

security_research.title: DKIM Canonicalization Attack Vectors
security_research.key_finding: Simple vs. relaxed canonicalization mismatches enable signature invalidation attacks

mitigations.title: Enforce DKIM Signing with ARC for Email Forwarding
mitigations.description: Implement Authenticated Received Chain (ARC) to preserve DKIM authentication results across email forwarding and mailing list hops. ARC seals each authentication result, creating a chain of custody for email provenance.

code_example.code:
# Install OpenDKIM and OpenARC
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install opendkim opendkim-tools openarc

# Generate DKIM keys
sudo mkdir -p /etc/opendkim/keys/example.com
sudo opendkim-genkey -b 2048 -d example.com -D /etc/opendkim/keys/example.com -s default -v
sudo chown opendkim:opendkim /etc/opendkim/keys/example.com/default.private

# Configure OpenDKIM (/etc/opendkim.conf)
cat <<EOF | sudo tee /etc/opendkim.conf
Syslog                  yes
UMask                   002
Mode                    sv
Canonical              relaxed/relaxed
OversignHeaders        From
TrustAnchorFile       /usr/share/dns/root.key
UserID                opendkim
Socket                inet:8891@localhost
PidFile               /var/run/opendkim/opendkim.pid
KeyTable              /etc/opendkim/key.table
SigningTable          /etc/opendkim/signing.table
InternalHosts         /etc/opendkim/trusted.hosts
EOF

# Configure key table
cat <<EOF | sudo tee /etc/opendkim/key.table
default._domainkey.example.com example.com:default:/etc/opendkim/keys/example.com/default.private
EOF

# Configure signing table
cat <<EOF | sudo tee /etc/opendkim/signing.table
*@example.com default._domainkey.example.com
EOF

# Configure OpenARC (/etc/openarc.conf)
cat <<EOF | sudo tee /etc/openarc.conf
Syslog                yes
Mode                  sv
AuthservID            example.com
Socket                inet:8893@localhost
PidFile               /var/run/openarc/openarc.pid
TrustAnchorFile       /usr/share/dns/root.key
Canonical             relaxed/relaxed
EnableCoredumps       yes
EOF

# Configure Postfix to use DKIM and ARC (/etc/postfix/main.cf)
sudo postconf -e 'smtpd_milters = inet:localhost:8891,inet:localhost:8893'
sudo postconf -e 'non_smtpd_milters = inet:localhost:8891,inet:localhost:8893'
sudo postconf -e 'milter_default_action = accept'
sudo postconf -e 'milter_protocol = 6'

# Restart services
sudo systemctl restart opendkim openarc postfix

# Publish DKIM public key in DNS (TXT record)
# default._domainkey.example.com
# v=DKIM1; k=rsa; p=MIIBIjANBgkqhki... (from default.txt)

# Publish ARC DNS record
# _arc.example.com TXT "v=ARC1"

# Test DKIM signing
echo "Test email" | mail -s "DKIM Test" test@example.com
sudo tail -f /var/log/mail.log | grep -i dkim

nist_csf: DE.AE-1 - A baseline of network operations is established
validation_steps: Send test email and verify DKIM-Signature header is present
Forward email through intermediate MTA and verify ARC-Seal header
Check DKIM signature validation at recipient with 'opendkim-testkey -d example.com -s default'
Verify ARC chain integrity with arc-milter tools

mitigations.title: Implement DMARC Policy with Forensic Reporting
mitigations.description: Deploy DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting & Conformance) with strict policy (p=reject) and forensic reporting (ruf) to prevent email delivery when DKIM fails and receive detailed failure reports.
validation_steps: Publish DMARC record with p=reject policy
Send test email and verify DKIM signature in headers
Check DMARC reports at rua email address
Verify forensic reports (ruf) are received for failures
Test email rejection when DKIM signature is missing

mitigations.title: Implement Email Forensic Logging with Amazon SES
mitigations.description: Enable comprehensive email event logging in Amazon SES with DKIM header preservation, storing authentication results in S3 for legal non-repudiation.
nist_csf: PR.PT-1 - Audit/log records are determined and documented
DE.AE-3 - Event data are collected and correlated
validation_steps: Enable DKIM signing in SES console
Create SNS topic for SES event notifications
Configure S3 bucket with Object Lock for compliance
Send test email and verify DKIM-Signature header
Check CloudWatch Logs for delivery events with DKIM results

mitigations.title: Implement DKIM Selector Key Rotation Policy
mitigations.description: Rotate DKIM private keys every 90 days and use multiple selectors (s1, s2) to enable zero-downtime key rotation, preventing key compromise attacks.

code_example.code:
#!/bin/bash
# DKIM Key Rotation Script
# Schedule with cron: 0 0 1 */3 * /usr/local/bin/rotate-dkim-keys.sh

set -euo pipefail

DOMAIN="example.com"
KEY_DIR="/etc/opendkim/keys/${DOMAIN}"
CURRENT_SELECTOR=$(cat "${KEY_DIR}/.current_selector")
NEW_SELECTOR="s$(($(date +%s) % 2 + 1))"  # Toggle between s1 and s2

echo "[$(date)] Starting DKIM key rotation for ${DOMAIN}"
echo "Current selector: ${CURRENT_SELECTOR}"
echo "New selector: ${NEW_SELECTOR}"

# Step 1: Generate new DKIM key pair
echo "Generating new 2048-bit RSA key..."
mkdir -p "${KEY_DIR}/${NEW_SELECTOR}"
opendkim-genkey -b 2048 -d "${DOMAIN}" -D "${KEY_DIR}/${NEW_SELECTOR}" -s "${NEW_SELECTOR}" -v
chown opendkim:opendkim "${KEY_DIR}/${NEW_SELECTOR}/${NEW_SELECTOR}.private"
chmod 600 "${KEY_DIR}/${NEW_SELECTOR}/${NEW_SELECTOR}.private"

# Step 2: Extract public key for DNS
PUBLIC_KEY=$(cat "${KEY_DIR}/${NEW_SELECTOR}/${NEW_SELECTOR}.txt" | grep -oP '(?<=p=)[^"]+' | tr -d ' \n')
echo "New public key: ${PUBLIC_KEY:0:50}..."

# Step 3: Update DNS record (Azure DNS example)
az network dns record-set txt add-record \
  --resource-group "email-prod-rg" \
  --zone-name "${DOMAIN}" \
  --record-set-name "${NEW_SELECTOR}._domainkey" \
  --value "v=DKIM1; k=rsa; p=${PUBLIC_KEY}"

echo "DNS record published: ${NEW_SELECTOR}._domainkey.${DOMAIN}"

# Step 4: Wait for DNS propagation (5 minutes)
echo "Waiting 5 minutes for DNS propagation..."
sleep 300

# Step 5: Verify DNS record
echo "Verifying DNS record..."
DNS_CHECK=$(dig +short TXT "${NEW_SELECTOR}._domainkey.${DOMAIN}" | tr -d '"')
if [[ "${DNS_CHECK}" == *"${PUBLIC_KEY:0:50}"* ]]; then
  echo "✓ DNS record verified"
else
  echo "✗ DNS record verification failed"
  exit 1
fi

# Step 6: Update OpenDKIM configuration
echo "Updating OpenDKIM key table..."
cat > "${KEY_DIR}/key.table" <<EOF
${NEW_SELECTOR}._domainkey.${DOMAIN} ${DOMAIN}:${NEW_SELECTOR}:${KEY_DIR}/${NEW_SELECTOR}/${NEW_SELECTOR}.private
EOF

# Update signing table
cat > "${KEY_DIR}/signing.table" <<EOF
*@${DOMAIN} ${NEW_SELECTOR}._domainkey.${DOMAIN}
EOF

# Step 7: Reload OpenDKIM (graceful restart)
echo "Reloading OpenDKIM service..."
systemctl reload opendkim

# Step 8: Test new DKIM signature
echo "Testing new DKIM signature..."
opendkim-testkey -d "${DOMAIN}" -s "${NEW_SELECTOR}" -k "${KEY_DIR}/${NEW_SELECTOR}/${NEW_SELECTOR}.private"

if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "✓ New DKIM key validated successfully"
  echo "${NEW_SELECTOR}" > "${KEY_DIR}/.current_selector"
  
  # Step 9: Archive old key (keep for 30 days for pending emails)
  if [ -n "${CURRENT_SELECTOR}" ] && [ -d "${KEY_DIR}/${CURRENT_SELECTOR}" ]; then
    ARCHIVE_DIR="${KEY_DIR}/archive/$(date +%Y%m%d)-${CURRENT_SELECTOR}"
    mkdir -p "${ARCHIVE_DIR}"
    mv "${KEY_DIR}/${CURRENT_SELECTOR}" "${ARCHIVE_DIR}/"
    echo "Old key archived: ${ARCHIVE_DIR}"
    
    # Schedule DNS record removal in 30 days
    echo "az network dns record-set txt delete --resource-group email-prod-rg --zone-name ${DOMAIN} --name ${CURRENT_SELECTOR}._domainkey --yes" | at now + 30 days
  fi
  
  echo "[$(date)] DKIM key rotation completed successfully"
else
  echo "✗ DKIM key validation failed"
  exit 1
fi

# Step 10: Send notification
curl -X POST "${SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL}" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d "{
    \"text\": \"DKIM key rotated for ${DOMAIN}\",
    \"attachments\": [{
      \"color\": \"good\",
      \"fields\": [
        {\"title\": \"Domain\", \"value\": \"${DOMAIN}\", \"short\": true},
        {\"title\": \"New Selector\", \"value\": \"${NEW_SELECTOR}\", \"short\": true},
        {\"title\": \"Rotation Date\", \"value\": \"$(date)\", \"short\": false}
      ]
    }]
  }"

echo "Notification sent to Slack"

nist_csf: PR.AC-1 - Identities and credentials are issued and managed
PR.IP-2 - A System Development Life Cycle is implemented
validation_steps: Test key rotation script in staging environment
Verify dual-selector support (s1 and s2) in OpenDKIM
Schedule cron job for quarterly rotation
Test email delivery with new DKIM selector

indicators: DKIM signature missing in received email headers (DKIM=none)
DKIM signature verification failure (DKIM=fail) with reason 'signature stripped'
ARC-Seal headers missing in forwarded emails
Increase in DMARC failure reports (ruf) for missing DKIM signatures
Emails passing SPF but failing DKIM and DMARC alignment
Multiple email bounces due to DMARC p=reject policy after DKIM failure

log_queries.query:
// Detect DKIM signature stripping in email logs
EmailEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(24h)
| extend DkimResult = tostring(parse_json(AuthenticationDetails).DKIM)
| where DkimResult in ("none", "fail", "temperror")
| extend SpfResult = tostring(parse_json(AuthenticationDetails).SPF)
| where SpfResult == "pass"  // SPF passes but DKIM fails = potential stripping
| summarize FailureCount = count() by SenderFromDomain, RecipientDomain, DkimResult, bin(Timestamp, 1h)
| where FailureCount > 10
| order by FailureCount desc
event.category: "email" AND email.authentication.dkim: ("none" OR "fail") AND email.authentication.spf: "pass" AND NOT email.headers.arc_seal: *

index=email_security sourcetype=mail_logs
| rex field=_raw "Authentication-Results:.*dkim=(?<dkim_result>\w+)"
| rex field=_raw "Authentication-Results:.*spf=(?<spf_result>\w+)"
| search dkim_result IN (none, fail, temperror) spf_result=pass
| stats count by sender_domain, recipient_domain, dkim_result, _time
| where count > 5
| sort -count

fields @timestamp, mail.messageId, mail.source, delivery.dkimSignatureVerdict, delivery.spfVerdict
| filter eventType = "Delivery"
| filter delivery.dkimSignatureVerdict != "PASS" and delivery.spfVerdict = "PASS"
| stats count() as failures by mail.source, delivery.dkimSignatureVerdict
| filter failures > 10
| sort failures desc
